coredata.dc:title: Glucose functionalized carbon quantum dot containing organic radical for optical/MR dual-modality bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Xiaodan
Zhang, Zhide
Xia, Qi
Hou, Meirong
Yan, Chenggong
Chen, Zelong
Xu, Yikai
Liu, Ruiyuan
coredata.dc:description: The organic paramagnetic compounds nitroxides have great potential as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents. Herein, we report the synthesis and characterization of glucose modified carbon quantum dot containing 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidinooxy (TEMPO) for targeted bimodal MR/optical imaging of tumor cells. CQD-TEMPO-Glu shows the greatest potentials for bioimaging applications in view of low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, green fluorescence emission and high T1 relaxivities. The in vitro MR and optical imaging results confirm enhanced cellular internalization of CQD-TEMPO-Glu in cancer cells through GLUT mediated endocytosis. These results confirm that CQD-TEMPO-Glu is expected to be widely exploited as dual-modal contrast for cancer imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Optical bioimaging
MR bioimaging
Glucose
GLUT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: T1 values measurement and T1 relaxation rate calculation
Cell culture and in vitro studies
T1 values measurement and T1 relaxation rate calculation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The IR spectra of CQD, CQD-TEMPO and CQD-TEMPO-Glu.
TEM image (a) and size distribution (b) of CQD-TEMPO-Glu.
UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of the CQD-TEMPO-Glu obtained at different excitation wavelengths.
The T1 relaxivity curve of CQD-TEMPO-Glu NPs phantoms.
Cell viability of the cells incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu for 24h or 48h

Fluorescence images (×200) of cells after cellular uptake CQD and histogram of fluorescence intensity (A: HepG2 incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu, B: HepG2 incubated with CQD-TEMPO, C:HL-7702 incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu, D: HL-7702 incubated with CQD-TEMPO, Bar scale:50μm).

Fluorescence intensity values of 5 groups after cellular uptake (measured by flow cytometry) and histogram (A:HepG2 incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu, B:HepG2 incubated with CQD-TEMPO, C:HL-7702 incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu, D:HL-7702 incubated with CQD-TEMPO).

a: T1-weighted MR images of cellular agarose gel tubes after cellular uptake CQD, b: Corresponding histogram of the standardized quantitative SNR values (A: HepG2 incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu, B: HepG2 incubated with CQD-TEMPO, C: HL-7702 incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu, D: HL-7702 incubated with CQD -TEMPO).
The fluorescence intensity of images of cells after cellular uptake CQD (measured by ImageJ software) and the pairwise comparison the results.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Meaned statistically significant differences with Blank group.
Meaned statistically significant differences with A group (P <0.001).

head.ce:title: Glucose functionalized carbon quantum dot containing organic radical for optical/MR dual-modality bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Imaging Center, Nanfang Hospital, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou 510515, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Medical Image Processing, School of Biomedical Engineering, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou 510515, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Medical Image Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou 510515, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: R. Liu, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Medical Image Processing, School of Biomedical Engineering, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou 510515, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Medical Image Processing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The organic paramagnetic compounds nitroxides have great potential as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents. Herein, we report the synthesis and characterization of glucose modified carbon quantum dot containing 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidinooxy (TEMPO) for targeted bimodal MR/optical imaging of tumor cells. CQD-TEMPO-Glu shows the greatest potentials for bioimaging applications in view of low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, green fluorescence emission and high T1 relaxivities. The in vitro MR and optical imaging results confirm enhanced cellular internalization of CQD-TEMPO-Glu in cancer cells through GLUT mediated endocytosis. These results confirm that CQD-TEMPO-Glu is expected to be widely exploited as dual-modal contrast for cancer imaging.

Glucose modified carbon quantum dot containing TEMPO (CQD-TEMPO-Glu) is expected to be exploited as targeted dual-modal contrast for cancer imaging, in view of low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, green fluorescence emission and high T1 relaxivities.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Hybrid materials and inorganic materials are upcoming materials in the biomedical sciences and engineering fields. The functional hybrid organic/inorganic nanocarriers have been widely explored in biosensors, drug and genetic materials delivery, [1–3] mediated optogenetics [4], near-infrared photodynamic therapy [5] and even the remotely controlled therapeutic delivery [6]. In addition, they are also used in purification and biomineralization of peptides [7,8], development of field emitters [9] and remotely actuated applications [10]. Very recently, the advances of atomic layer deposition technology further expanded their potential application areas [11].

The widely used magnetic resonance (MR) contrast agents enhance MR signal and improve diagnosis in clinical practice. Nowadays, Gadolinium (Gd) based contrast agents (GBCAs) have accounted for essentially all of the MR contrast agents used in clinical enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans [12]. However, it was gradually reported that GBCAs administration may lead to accumulation of toxic free Gd in important tissues, even associate with the development of nephrogenic systemic fibrosis [13–15]. Nitroxide stable free radicals (NSFR)are paramagnetic organic compounds with a single unpaired electron, which can shorten T1 and r2/r1 and provide bright contrast. As early as eighties of last century, NSFR have already been reported could be used as intravenous MR contrast agents in addition to paramagnetic metals [16–19]. A series of related experiments [20–23] in vitro and in vivo showed that nitroxyl spin labels possessed some favorable features similar to GBCAs, such as electron paramagnetism, fast renal elimination, long half-life and chemical versatility, but metal-free and relatively low toxicity. MR contrast agents containing 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine 1-oxyl (TEMPO) have been successfully synthesized and evaluated in our previous work [24]. These metal-free organic paramagnetic contrast agents may be a promising choice.

The fluorescent materials with features of low toxicity and high efficiency were often applied in various labeling, imaging and therapeutic studies for gene delivery [2,25], photodynamic therapy [5], targeted photothermal ablation [26,27], bioimaging and biodetection [28,29] and the detection of organophosphorus pesticides [30]. In recent years, the focuses of the development of novel MR contrast agents had not only been the improvement of 1H water relaxivities and water dispersibility, but also the achievement of multimodal imaging. Conjugation with conventional fluorescent dye or fluorescein had been used to build the multimodal contrast agents in our previous work [24,31], but we still suffered from their stubborn disadvantages. Very recently, fluorescent carbon quantum dot (CQD) has attracted much our attention. Compared with the conventional dyes, the key advantages of non-blinking photoluminescence and excellent photostability make CQD in favor of single-molecule tracking and real-time imaging [32]. Similar to the toxic and poorly soluble semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), non-metallic CQD also have the properties including low cost and easy to synthesis, small size and high biological compatibility, excellent light stability and height adjustable photoluminescence, chemical inertness and easy for functionalization [32–34]. The good fluorescence properties and the robust platform for surface specific modification provided by CQD were much conducive to synthesis of multifunctional agents. We noted that many biological applications of functional CQD including bioimaging, biosensing and drug/gene delivery have been reported [35–37], but used for MR contrast medium developing was relatively less. This time, through a good integration between CQD and NSFR with the potential of chemical bonding [22], we attempted to design CQD modified with TEMPO to act as T1-enhanced, fluorescent contrasted and non-metallic bimodal MR contrast agents.

Choosing the appropriate targeting ligands may contribute to the maximization of molecular probe performance. Glucose transport (GLUT) is a relatively well-studied carrier mediating sugar intake in various normal and tumor cells [38–41]. The classic “Warburg effect” indicated that malignant tumor cells with high metabolism more depend on the aerobic glycolysis, which means more glucose consumption and over-expression of GLUTs compared to normal cells, especially the GLUT-1 [42–46]. Based on the theory, targeting GLUTs were often considered in the developments of the targeted probes and anti-cancer drug [47,48]. Thus, we supposed that the MR contrast agents modified with glucose may be more sensitive and significant detection by the GLUTs over-expressing tumor cells.

In this work, CQD prepared via a simple reaction of a microwave-assisted method were further modified with TEMPO and glucose to be the GLUT-targeting MRI/optical imaging bimodal contrast agents (CQD-TEMPO-Glu). The basic physicochemical properties, cytotoxicity and uptake specificity of the agents by GLUTs-overexpressing on human liver cancer HepG2 cells in vitro were reported and compared to the normal human liver HL-7702 cells. Furthermore, the glucose-mediated cancer specific uptake efficacy of the CQD-TEMPO-Glu was also evaluated by MRI and optical imaging in vitro.

All reagents and solvents were purchased from commercial sources. Solvents were dried according to standard procedures. Deionized water (DI water) was used to prepare all the aqueous solutions. Citric acid, thiourea, 4-amine-2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidinooxy (4-amine-TEMPO), D-Glucosamine hydrochloride (GAH), triethylamine, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (Sulfo-NHS) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Adamas (China). Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), paraformaldehyde and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (USA).

The size and morphology of the synthesized NPs were observed by a HITACHI H-7650 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 200kV. The mean size distribution of the particles was measured using a Malvern Zeta-size 3000HS. IR spectra were measured using a Shimadzu FTIR-8100 spectrophotometer. Absorbance detections were carried out on Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorometer with a xenon lamp and 1.0cm quartz cells.

The carboxyl-terminated CQD were synthesized according to methods described in the literature with some modifications [49]. In brief, thiourea (0.03g, 0.5mmol) and citric acid (0.96g, 5mmol) were dissolved in distilled water (10mL) and stirred well. The mixture was microwave-assisted reacted at 720W for 5min. After precipitation with MeOH and centrifugal separation, the carboxyl-terminated CQD were obtained by vacuum drying. Finally, CQD were dispersed in purified water at a concentration of 1.0mg/mL for subsequent experiments.

The carboxyl-terminated CQD (200mg), then EDC·HCl (80mg) and Sulfo-NHS (80mg) were added to 10mL DI water and stirred for 1h. The 4-amine-TEMPO (60mg) in DMSO (5mL) was added in the above carboxyl-terminated CQD solvent and stirred for 48h at room temperature. The pure product was obtained by dialysis against DI water for 48h using a tubular membrane of cellulose (MWCO 1500Da). The residual suspension was finally freeze-dried to give CQD-TEMPO.

CQD-TEMPO (100mg) were dissolved in 5mL DI water, EDC·HCl (30mg) and Sulfo-NHS (30mg) were added and stirred for 1h. Then GAH (10mg) and triethylamine (50μL) were added in the solvent and reacted for 48h at room temperature. The pure product was obtained by dialysis against DI water for 48h using a tubular membrane of cellulose (MWCO 1500Da). The residual suspension was finally freeze-dried to give CQD-TEMPO-Glu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: T1 values measurement and T1 relaxation rate calculation
ce:section.ce:para: CQD-TEMPO-Glu was prepared at the concentration of 0, 1.25, 2.5, 5, 10mg/mL in 5mL Eppendorf tubes respectively. The T1 of these solutions were determined by Philips Achieva 3.0T MR scanner using a T1 mapping sequence. The scan parameters of the sequence were as follows: TR:3000ms,TE:10ms,IR:50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000ms; slices thickness: 4mm; gap: 0.5mm; field of view: 52.491; NSA: 2. Images of the phantoms were analyzed by defining regions of interest (ROI) in each test tube. Relaxivity (r1) was calculated via curve-fitting of T1 (s−1) vs the CQD-TEMPO-Glu concentration (mg/mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and in vitro studies

ce:section.ce:para: All the cell culture supplies were purchased from Corning (USA) and Life Technologies (USA). Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Gibco (USA). Human liver cancer HepG2 cell lines and normal human liver HL-7702 cell lines were gifted from the Research Center of Clinical Medicine in Nanfang Hospital (Guangzhou, China). HepG2 cell lines were cultured in DMEM, supplemented with 10% FBS, penicillin (100U/mL), and streptomycin (100U/mL), and HL-7702 cell lines were supplemented in the same medium besides with 20% FBS. All the cells were incubated at 37°C with 5% CO2 in atmosphere.
The solutions of CQD-TEMPO-Glu and CQD-TEMPO were dissolved in DI water and further diluted with culture medium to be an appropriate concentration using in the following cellular experiments.

HepG2 cells and HL-7702 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at 6500 cells/well and co-incubated until the cell confluence reached 80%. Subsequently, the old culture medium was replaced by 200μL medium containing CQD-TEMPO-Glu at different concentrations (1.25, 2.5, 10, 20mg/mL) and the cells were kept incubation for 24h or 48h. Six replicate wells were used for one concentration. After incubation, the medium was removed and 200μL medium containing MTT (5mg/mL) was added into and incubated for another 4h. The formazan crystals in each well was dissolved by 150μL DMSO and completely dissolved via being shaken on tablet oscillator for 15min, then the absorbance was measured at 490nm wavelength by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay reader BIOTEK ELX80. The relative cell viability (%) related to control wells containing no nanoparticle was calculated by [[A]test−[A]blank control]/[[A]untreated−[A]blank control]×100%.

In order to be better contrast, we divided experimental groups and blank control group according to the cells and the co-incubated CQD. The experimental groups were as follows: (A) HepG2 cells co-incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu; (B) HepG2 cells co-incubated with CQD-TEMPO; (C) HL-7702 cells co-incubated with CQD -TEMPO-Glu; (D) HL-7702 cells co-incubated with CQD-TEMPO; (Blank) Blank control. The subsequent experiments were also carried out according to the same grouping.

The cells were seeded onto the 48-well plate with 2–3×104 cells/well and incubated overnight. The medium of experimental groups were replaced with 500μL culture medium containing 2mg/mL CQD-TEMPO-Glu or CQD-TEMPO respectively. The Blank group was replaced with new fresh medium conventionally. After 4h co-incubation, cells of each group were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS), fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 15min and counterstained cell nucleus with DAPI for 5min. Then, the fixed cells were imaged using an Olympus Ix71 inverted fluorescence microscope. Background correction and image merger were implemented using the accompanied DPManager software, and the measurement of fluorescence intensity was implemented by ImageJ software [Rasband, WS. ImageJ. US National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, Maryland, USA. http://imagej.nih.gov/ij/;1997–2014].

Statistical Analysis: SPSS 13.0 statistical package was used as analyzing software. All data are evaluated as means±standard deviation. One-way ANOVA was used to analyze the difference of fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent images of each group (α=0.05, P <0.05 were considered as significant difference). Post hoc multiple comparisons were used LSD method or Dunnett's T3 method.

HepG2 cells and HL-7702 cells were seeded in 6-well culture plates at 5–6×105 cells/well. When cell confluence reached to 90%, the old culture medium in experimental groups was replaced by new medium containing corresponding NPs. After 4h co-incubation, the liquid in all the wells was removed and the cells were rinsed with PBS twice. The cells were digested and centrifuged for collection according to groups. Subsequently, a suspension of staining cells in 500μL PBS was prepared and measured by flow cytometry for fluorescence intensity (BD FACSAria™ III, USA). The instrument was calibrated with non-treated blank cells (negative control) to identify viable cells.

The HepG2 cells and HL-7702 cells grouped as A (HepG2 incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu), B (HepG2 incubated with CQD-TEMPO), C (HL-7702 incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu) and D (HL-7702 incubated with CQD-TEMPO) were proliferated in 10cm cells petri dishes until the cell confluence reached 90%. The concentration of CQD-TEMPO-Glu or CQD-TEMPO in co-incubated medium containing is 8mg/mL. The blank control group was replaced with new fresh medium as the previous experiments. After the incubation incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu or CQD-TEMPO, The cells of each group were treated as followed: washing, digestion with trypsin, and collection as previous experiment, these procedures were repeated 3 times. Then the cells were resuspended with 180μL 1% agarose gel in Eppendorf tubes. Pure 1% agarose gel was used as a control.

The T1-weighted MR images were obtained using the clinical 3.0T Philips Achieva scanner with an 8 channels head coil. Images of cells suspension in 180μL 1% agarose gel grouped by A (HepG2 incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu), B(HepG2 incubated with CQD-TEMPO), C (HL-7702 incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu), D (HL-7702 incubated with CQD-TEMPO) and Blank (blank control) were collected by a TSE sequence (flip angle 90°, TR/TE 500/10ms, slice thickness 4mm, gap 0mm, FOV: 83.33, NSA 8). Subsequent postprocessing was as follows: ROIs were delineated in the central region of the tubes images scanned at the same sections and the signal intensity (SI) and noise was measured. Signal noise ratios (SNR) were calculated according to the following formula: SNR=SI/noise. The measurements and calculations of ROIs were repeated three times for the averages.

Statistical Analysis: SPSS 13.0 statistical package was used as analyzing software. All enumeration data were evaluated as means±standard deviation. One way ANOVA was used to analyze the difference of MR signal intensity of each group. (α=0.05, P <0.05 was considered as significant difference).

Since carboxyl groups are able to facilitate the dehydration and carbonization, citric acid is frequently used as carbon source to prepare CQD. In this study, the carboxyl-coated CQD were prepared by a microwave-assisted reaction with citric acid as the carbon source. Afterwards, MRI report group TEMPO and glucose were coupled onto the surface of CQD which is shown in Scheme 1 .

FTIR spectrum of the CQD was shown in Fig. 1 . The sharp peaks at about 3433cm−1 and 1400cm−1 were assigned to the OH stretching vibration and OH bending vibration, respectively. The peak at 1707cm−1 was associated with the asymmetric stretching vibration of the carboxylate anions. Additionally, the peaks at 1362 and 1072cm−1come from the CN and CS vibration bonds, respectively. The peaks at 1640cm−1 and 1384cm−1 of CQD-TEMPO and CQD-TEMPO-Glu were possibly indicative of the CO stretching vibration and CN stretching vibrations, confirming the newly formed amide bonds between NH2-TEMPO or NH2-Glucose and CQD. Similarly, the peak at 2949cm−1 which is attributed to the N-oxyl stretching vibration was also observed, suggesting the successful conjugation of TEMPO onto the surface of CQD.

Fig. 2 shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and size distribution of CQD-TEMPO-Glu, which indicated that CQD-TEMPO-Glu is monodispersed and uniform with spherical structures. It was shown that the average particles size is distributed in a relatively narrow range. The distribution of particle size is concentrated in 5–12nm, the average particle size is about 8nm. DLS measurements revealed that CQD-TEMPO-Glu exhibits with a narrow size distribution between 5 and 10nm.

CQD-TEMPO-Glu was highly dispersible in water, and exhibited a long-term stability without any noticeable precipitation at room temperature. The optical properties of CQD-TEMPO-Glu were investigated by UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Consistent with previously reported CQD, a broad absorption band with no distinct peaks in the region from 300 to 650nm was presented in the UV–Vis spectra of CQD-TEMPO-Glu (Fig. 3 ).

CQD-TEMPO-Glu exhibited a characteristic wavelength dependent emission, where the emission peak shifted from 450 to 520nm under 270–450nm excitation (Fig. 3). This excitation-dependent PL emission has been commonly observed in CQD and has been ascribed to excitation of different sized CQD or conjugated sp2 domains in the CQD carbon network. The aqueous solution of CQD-TEMPO-Glu presented bright green photoluminescence centered at 505nm in the emission spectrum under the radiation at the max excitation wavelength of 368nm. No apparent wavelength shift in FL spectra of CQD, CQD-TEMPO, and CQD-TEMPO-Glu was observed in their emission spectra (Fig. S1), suggesting the optical properties of CQD are not perturbed. The fluorescence stability of CQD-TEMPO-Glu in biological medium was also monitored (Fig. S2). After incubation at 37°C for 10d in PBS, CQD-TEMPO-Glu possesses 98% of its initial fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: T1 values measurement and T1 relaxation rate calculation
ce:section.ce:para: TEMPO modification on the surface of CQD renders CQD the MRI modality. The T1 contrast capability of CQD-TEMPO-Glu was investigated on a clinical 3.0T MRI instrument. To evaluate the MRI contrast effect of CQD-TEMPO-Glu, a phantom study was performed with various CQD-TEMPO-Glu gradient concentrations. The T1-weighted image of the different phantoms and the curve-fitting calculated based on them with the post-processing were showed in Fig. 4 , The linear regression equation obtained by curve fitting was y=0.250×+0.467, it means that the relaxivity (r1) of CQD-TEMPO-Glu is 0.25mg−1 s−1.

The cytotoxicity of CQD-TEMPO-Glu was evaluated by MTT cell viability assay. The HepG2 cells and HL-7702 cells were co-cultured with CQD for 24h and 48h. As shown in Fig. 5 , with CQD-TEMPO-Glu concentration increasing, whether tumor cells or normal cells, though presented a mild downward trend, the cells viability all reached at least 75% even at the highest concentration of CQD-TEMPO-Glu. The results indicated that CQD-TEMPO-Glu exhibits no significant cytotoxicity and possesses relatively good biocompatibility at the given concentrations.

To demonstrate the feasibility of CQD-TEMPO-Glu for fluorescent cell imaging, HepG2 cells were incubated with 2mg/mL CQD-TEMPO-Glu and visualized under inverted fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 6a, CQD-TEMPO-Glu could quickly penetrate the cells and accumulate in the cytoplasm region to give bright green fluorescence. Moreover, the stained cells grow healthy and displayed their normal morphology demonstrating that CQD-TEMPO-Glu is biocompatible.

Glucose is an important molecule for cells that use it as an energy substrate. The need for high metabolic consumption of glucose in cancers is related to the fact that many cancers employ a high-rate glycolytic pathway, i.e., the use of glucose to generate lactic acid by cancer cells despite the availability of oxygen for oxidative phosphorylation, a phenomenon commonly known as the Warburg effect [42–46]. Hence, a high uptake of glucose and increased metabolic rates are necessary to maintain the proliferation of the cancer cells. To confirm the selective uptake of glucose modified CQD into cancer cells over normal cells, we measured the in vitro fluorescence imaging performances of CQD-TEMPO-Glu and CQD-TEMPO in tumor cells (HepG2) and normal cells (HL-7702).

As shown in Fig. 6, HepG2 cells or HL-7702 cells incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu or CQD-TEMPO exhibited green fluorescence in cell with different fluorescence intensity. On the contrary, there was no green fluorescence in Blank group. The intensity via visual inspection of HepG2 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO-Glu is higher than that HepG2 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO, HL-7702 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO-Glu, and HL-7702 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO. We also measured the fluorescence intensity value of HepG2 cells or HL-7702 cells incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu or CQD-TEMPO using the ImageJ software for a more accurate comparison as shown in Table 1 . The specific data of the fluorescence intensity of HepG2 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO-Glu (0.0693) is higher than that HepG2 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO (0.0463), HL-7702 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO-Glu (0.0394), and HL-7702 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO (0.0191).

The significant differences results might indicate the effects of specific binding or collaborative mediating of glucose on the surface of CQD-TEMPO-Glu for the overexpressing HepG2 cells had worked, which facilitated the enhanced cellular uptake of CQD-TEMPO-Glu.

The flow cytometry analysis was also carried out to evaluate the cell internalization of CQD-TEMPO-Glu and CQD-TEMPO by HepG2 cells or HL-7702 cells. As shown in Fig. 7 , the average fluorescence intensity of HepG2 cells labeled by CQD-TEMPO-Glu is much higher than that HepG2 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO (2.4-fold), HL-7702 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO-Glu (1.5-fold), and HL-7702 cells stained by CQD-TEMPO (2.8-fold), revealing the higher cellular uptake of CQD-TEMPO-Glu by HepG2 cells. The cytometry examination appeared a trend consistent with the previous test results provided by fluorescent images.

The applicability and the T1-enhanced effect of NPs internalized into the cells were investigated using in vitro cellular MRI. As shown in Fig. 8 , the T1-weighted signal of HepG2 cells incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu agarose suspension is higher than that HepG2 cells incubated with CQD-TEMPO, HL-7702 cells incubated with CQD-TEMPO-Glu, and HL-7702 cells incubated with CQD-TEMPO), it was suggested that CQD-TEMPO-Glu are most taken by HepG2 cells and provide the most significant T1-enhanced effect. The results demonstrate that CQD-TEMPO-Glu can provide a good T1 contrast enhanced at the cellular level which be mainly benefit by GULTs-mediated internalization. For another, nitroxide radicals (TEMPO) have demonstrated their potential of enhancing cell permeability and exhibit a favorable volume distribution [50,51]and to be a promising contrast agent as proved in our previous work [24].

In conclusion, through simple microwave-assisted synthesis and chemical modifications, we have successfully prepared a fluorescent paramagnetic bimodal contrast agent CQD-TEMPO-Glu can be using in optical/MR imaging. The bimodal contrast agent possesses good biocompatibility, strong fluorescence emission and acceptable T1 enhanced effect. Conjugation with glucose made CQD-TEMPO-Glu be taken more by GULTs over-expressing HepG2 cells, and resulted in the visible enough signal changes of optical imaging and MR imaging in vitro. Our pre-work may validate the feasibility of multi-modality organic radical contrast agents via favorable integration of novel fluorescent materials and organic paramagnetic compound, and open up new perspectives for specific multi-modality tumor imaging.

This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81271642, No. 31371009, and No. 81671749), National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFC0107104), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong, China (No. 2016A030313546), and Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province, China (No. 2015B010131011 and No. 2015B020233019).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Materials Science & Engineering C-Materials For Biological Applications
